coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of novel carbon dots@metal organic framework composite for remarkable and highly sustained oxygen evolution reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Rehman, Muhammad Yousaf ur
Manzoor, Sumaira
Nazar, Nosheen
Abid, Abdul Ghafoor
Qureshi, Ashfaq Mahmood
Chughtai, Adeel Hussain
Joya, Khurram Saleem
Shah, Afzal
Ashiq, Muhammad Naeem
coredata.dc:description: Craving to search sources of energy is constantly compelling the mankind to find a source that is renewable or has enormous reservoirs and at the same time it poses no hazardous effect to mankind. Today’s energy deficient world has been seeking for an electrocatalyst which has a simple and facile synthesis strategy with marvellous catalytic performance and has outstandingly sustained stability for the highly lethargic Oxygen Evolution Reaction (OER). Here, a Ni-based mixed linker Metal Organic Framework (MOF) was synthesized using 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid (BTC) and 4,4`-bipyridine (bpy) as organic linkers. Carbon Dots (CDs) were incorporated into MOF during synthesis to impart synergistic effect to the as-synthesized composite. All the synthesized samples i.e CDs, MOF and CDs@MOF were characterized by various techniques to confirm the structural, compositional, morphological and textural analysis. The synthesized composite possessed rods like morphology with uniform distribution of CDs which offered superb stability for almost 40 hrs, large electrochemical active surface area (ECSA), least charge transfer resistance (Rct), high turnover frequency (TOF), reasonably low onset potential, low overpotential and a comparably low Tafel slope. The electrocatalyst owed its great catalytic efficiency and substantial stability due to the synergistic effect between CDs and MOF. This work is an effort to exploit the fabulous potential of MOF based nano-composite towards water splitting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Metal organic framework (MOF)
Composite
Oxygen evolution reaction (OER)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors (M. Y. Rehman and M. N. Ashiq) acknowledge Bahauddin Zakariya University, Multan Pakistan for financial support via grant no. DR&EL/D-545 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of a) CDs b) MOF and c) CDs@MOF with its inset (# and * represent CDs and MOF respectively) for phase structure of the synthesized samples.
FTIR spectra of (a) BTC, (b) MOF, (c) CDs and (d) CDs@MOF.
SEM micrographs with corresponding insets revealing enlarged view of a) CDs, b) MOF, and c) CDs@MOF nano-composite.
(a) Cyclic voltammograms of all materials (b) Tafel Slope analysis of bare NF, MOF, CDs and CDs@MOF.
Electrochemical impedance spectra of a) CDs, b) MOF and c) CDs@MOFnano-composite.
a) Comparison of cyclic voltammogram of CDs@MOF composite operated at a scan rate of 50 mVs−1 in 1 M KOH for the 1st, 500th and 1,000th cycle, b) Chronoamperometery stability curve.
Electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) of a) CDs, b) MOF and c) CDs@MOFnano-composite.
The grain size, BET surface area, pore size and pore volume for MOF and CDs@MOF nano-composite.
A comparison of catalytic performances of various MOF based electrocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of novel carbon dots@metal organic framework composite for remarkable and highly sustained oxygen evolution reaction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Craving to search sources of energy is constantly compelling the mankind to find a source that is renewable or has enormous reservoirs and at the same time it poses no hazardous effect to mankind. Today’s energy deficient world has been seeking for an electrocatalyst which has a simple and facile synthesis strategy with marvellous catalytic performance and has outstandingly sustained stability for the highly lethargic Oxygen Evolution Reaction (OER). Here, a Ni-based mixed linker Metal Organic Framework (MOF) was synthesized using 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid (BTC) and 4,4`-bipyridine (bpy) as organic linkers. Carbon Dots (CDs) were incorporated into MOF during synthesis to impart synergistic effect to the as-synthesized composite. All the synthesized samples i.e CDs, MOF and CDs@MOF were characterized by various techniques to confirm the structural, compositional, morphological and textural analysis. The synthesized composite possessed rods like morphology with uniform distribution of CDs which offered superb stability for almost 40 hrs, large electrochemical active surface area (ECSA), least charge transfer resistance (Rct), high turnover frequency (TOF), reasonably low onset potential, low overpotential and a comparably low Tafel slope. The electrocatalyst owed its great catalytic efficiency and substantial stability due to the synergistic effect between CDs and MOF. This work is an effort to exploit the fabulous potential of MOF based nano-composite towards water splitting.

ce:section.ce:para: Humanity on earth has been facing three huge challenges for the last few decades i.e. energy crises, rapidly deteriorating environmental condition and resultantly the economy issues. Interestingly, all these problems are interrelated to one another. Majority of human race consume fossil fuels to fulfil their energy needs. Initially the consumers were ignorant of the scarcity of the fossil fuels and also of their perilous effect on the environment. Aggravation in scarcity led to unparalleled inflation and unabated use caused unsurpassed environmental degradation. Scientists suggested the solution in probing an alternate source of energy that must be sustainable and have no hazardous effects on environment in any way [1–6]. Solar, Tidal, Wind, and Hydro energies were considered and harnessed to provide the humanity with affluent and inexpensive energy but none of these was devoid of drawbacks. So, researchers diverted their attention towards chemical energy and came to know that H2 is the cleanest and purest form of energy and has the potential to surpass all the contemporary sources in terms of efficiency and availability. Exploring various sources of H2 was the next level of research [7–11]. During the course of research, it was found that H2 obtained from water splitting has various advantages like raw material i.e. H2O was almost 71% on earth. It means it is matchlessly in abundance than anything else. Secondly, the H2 produced from that procedure is uncontaminated and the cleanest. Thirdly, it is eco-friendly, as the only by-product formed during its consumption is water again. All these interpretations compelled the researchers to divert their attentions towards this novel and dynamic avenue [12–15].

Basically, the water oxidation comprised of two half-cell reactions i.e. Oxygen Evolution Reaction (OER) and Hydrogen Evolution Reaction (HER). Thermodynamic and Kinetic studies of these reactions revealed that these are slothful reactions and require a high potential of 1.23 V to proceed. A wide variety of catalysts such as metal oxides [16,17], metal selenides [18], metal tellurides [7], metal sulphides [19], metal phosphides [20], MOF etc. were employed to overcome the energy barriers of that reaction. It was discovered that Pt and oxides of Ir and Ru showed greater catalytic activity to overcome the energy obstacles of HER and OER, respectively. But problem continued to exist, as these catalysts belonged to noble metals which were not cost effective. Their sources were inadequate and they were the most precious among the metals. These factors prohibited their use on commercial scale. Under these circumstances, an expedition to fabricate an earth abundant, eco-friendly, robust, cost effective catalyst, which not only have the potential to replace these noble metal catalysts but also have the tendency to surpass their catalytic activity, is on the way [21–24].

Carbon is a royal element and exists in various allotropic forms like diamond, graphite etc. It also has huge variety of nano-materials which include carbon dots, carbon nano-fibres, nano-tubes, nano-diamonds, fullerene and graphene etc. Carbon dots are spherical in shape, having size< 10 nm [25]. Carbon nano-fibers have cylindrical structures with graphene layers arranged as stacked cones, cups or plates [26]. When these graphene layers are wrapped into perfect cylinders, carbon nano-fibers are called carbon nano-tubes [27]. Carbon nano-diamonds are truncated octahedral architectures ranging from 2 to 8 nm in diameter. In fullerene, carbon atoms are connected via single or double bonds to form a closed or semi-closed structure with fused rings of five to seven atoms [28]. Graphene consists of a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a two-dimensional honeycomb lattice [29]. All these materials have been exploited for wide range of explorations and splendid results have been exhibited by them [30]. Among all, carbon dots (CDs) represent an innovative and diversified class of carbon materials with nano-size range and unusual fluorescent and catalytic features [31]. These have established their unique characteristics which are far superior and magnificent than their counterparts. They are extraordinarily biocompatible and non-toxic materials which have been exclusively employed in bio-imaging probes [32]. These wonderful and impressive merits have expanded the scope of CDs beyond mere bio-imaging probes to other dynamic fields of biomedicines including bio-sensing, drug delivery, gene delivery [33], as excellent theranostic agent [34], for carrying anti-cancer drugs [35] and in sensing many biological and non-biological molecules [36]. CDs have also played crucial part to demonstrate magnificent bacterial imaging, antibacterial activity [37] and in catalysis [38,39].

Metal Organic Frameworks (MOFs) constitute a unique arena of materials with wide range of applications. Metal ions and organic linkers are the leading constituent ingredients of these promising materials. MOFs are widely probed due to their highly ordered structures, sumptuous porosities, diversified surface, resultantly they possess very distinctive and dynamic properties [40,41]. Amazingly, their characteristics are tuneable and can be sculpted to the desired one, either by careful selection of the materials or by vigilant handling of the technique [42,43]. Versatile MOFs have found their efficacy in broad array of executions that include adsorption [44], sensors [45], gas storage [46], gas separation [47], drugs loading [48], photocatalytic reduction of CO2 [49], electrochemical applications [50–52] and in searching the alternative source of energy [53]. MOFs can be used in their pristine form in variety of fields, as template for the growth of various attractive materials and also as precursors for the synthesis of numerous vital substances whose synthesis would have been difficult otherwise. The tuneable porosity and rigid framework of MOFs also allow other stable materials like metal oxides, metal chalcogenide etc., to be incorporated into them to have combined and boosted activity [54]. Recently, many researchers have successfully tried calcinations with MOFs to get metals embedded into the carbon structures and have employed them in a variety of applications with tremendous and loftier results [55–58].

In this study, we have fabricated a novel MOF comprising of two ligands i.e. 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid (BTC) and 4,4`-bipyridine (bpy) and nickel nitrate hexahydrate [Ni (NO3)2•6H2O] as a Ni source. CDs were incorporated into the MOF to get the composite. It was revealed that our target material was found to exhibit brilliant OER activity in alkaline medium due to the synergistic effect of CDs@MOF. CDs based MOF composite loaded on NF showed an onset potential of 1.50 V vs RHE, the overpotential 320 mV at current density of 10 mV cm−2. Further, the as-prepared composite offered low resistance in the charge transfer and dominated with marvellous stability for 1,000 CV cycles and almost negligible current decay for continuous 40 hrs during chronoamperometric analysis.

Carbon Dots (CDs) were prepared via the greenroute developed by Li Wang et al. [59],. In that procedure, 25 mL of milk was added in 20 mL of water and stirred vigorously for fifteen minutes. The mixture was then transferred into 50 mL stainless steel autoclave and then kept in the oven at 180 °C for 2 hrs. When the reaction time was completed, the solution was allowed to cool down to room temperature naturally. When it reached the room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged at 13,000 rpm. The precipitates were washed several times with de-ionized water and finally with ethanol. The obtained material was dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C for 18 hrs. After drying, we obtained brown precipitates of CDs which were stored in desiccator for further process.

A mixed linker MOF was synthesized using a solvothermal process with slight modification in already reported method [60]. 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylicacid (BTC, 0.42 g, 2 mmol) was added into 40 mL of NN´-dimethylformamide (DMF). The solution was kept on stirring until a clear solution was obtained. To this solution, 4,4´-bipyridine (bpy, 0.47 g, 3 mmol) was added with continuous stirring. When it was completely dissolved, nickel nitrate hexahydrate [Ni (NO3)2•6H2O, 1.16 g, (4 mmol)] was added into it. Vigorous stirring continued for 15 min. The solution mixture was then shifted into a stainless-steel autoclave. After closing it tightly, it was put into the oven at 100 °C for 48 hrs. After, the time was over, it was cooled down to room temperature. The MOF mixture was further washed with the addition of some DMF during filtration using Whatman filter paper. The precipitates were dried at room temperature for overnight. The material was then shifted into the sample vial and saved for further process.

During the synthesis of MOF, when 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylicacid (BTC, 0.42 g, 2 mmol) and 4,4´-bipyridine (bpy, 0.47 g, 3 mmol) were dissolved into 40 mL of NN´-dimethylformamide (DMF), nickel nitrate hexahydrate [(Ni(NO3)2•6H2O,1.16 g, (4 mmol)] and CDs (0.8 g) were added to the solution for the preparation of the CDs@MOF composite. Rest of the procedure was same as mentioned above for the synthesis of MOF. The dried composite was saved for characterization and investigated for oxygen evolution reaction (OER).

To investigate the crystal structure of the as-synthesized materials, Powder X-ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out on a Bruker Diffractometer (D8-advance) with Ni-filter and used Cu-Kα radiations. The morphology of the materials was elucidated via Scanning Electron Microscope (JSM5910) with an accelerating voltage of 20 kV. The elemental analysis was carried out using energy dispersive spectrometer (JSM5910). The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area, pore size and pore volume of the prepared material was elucidated using NOVA2200e. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) was obtained on a Nicolet 170SXFTIR spectrometer (400–4,000 cm−1).

In a typical three electrodes system, reference (Ag/AgCl), counter (Pt wire) and working electrodes were used. In this study, we used nickel foam (NF) deposited with the synthesized materials as the working electrode. The substrate i.e.NF in this case, was cut into 1 cm × 1 cm pieces. Those pieces were sonicated for 15 min in acetone, HCl (2 M), de-ionized water and ethanol, respectively. After sonication, NF pieces were kept in oven at 60 °C for 30 min for drying. To study the electrochemical behaviour of the fabricated material, 1.5 mg of the material was first ground finely. De-ionized water (100 µL) was added to the powdered material. The solution was then ultra-sonicated for 1 hrs to get the homogenous catalyst ink. Drop-Casting method was employed to deposit the as prepared catalyst ink onto the already cleaned and dried NF. The as prepared catalytic ink (10 µL) was placed on the selected surface of the NF pieces which were then dried at room temperature. The dried loaded NF pieces were later employed for the studies of OER.

Electrochemical studies were carried out on a computer-controlled AUTOLAB Potentiostat (PGSTAT-204). It contained a typical three electrodes system hanged vertically in a Teflon-covered Pyrex glass cell. Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), Linear Sweep Voltammetry (LSV), Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) and Chronoamperometry (CA) were performed by this system to study the prepared materials for OER. A solution with a ratio of 1:3 of H2SO4 and HNO3 was employed for the cleaning of the electrochemical cell before it was put into operation. The cell was boiled in that solution followed by boiling in deionized water. After that, it was rinsed several times with deionized water and acetone, respectively. It was then placed in oven for drying at 80 °C. The counter electrode (Pt wire) was also made clean by washing it with plenty of de-ionized water and then immersing it in 20% solution of HNO3 for 5 min. A silver-silver chloride electrode (Ag/AgCl) containing the saturated solution of KCl was used as the reference electrode. 1 M KOH solution with a pH value of 13.6 was the electrolyte to carry out all the electrochemical measurements. All the readings were taken at room temperature. The solution was purged with argon gas for 20 min before use. The CV plots were taken deliberately at the +Ve as well as the –Ve potential at a scan rate of 10 mVs−1. As, complete NF electrode was not dipped in the electrolyte, so, all the obtained values were corrected considering the dipped geometrical surface area of the working electrode.

The potential obtained vs Ag/AgCl was changed to the potential of Reversible Hydrogen Electrode (RHE). Following correlation was used to present the data in RHE:
(1)
E
RHE = E
Ag/AgCl
+ 0·059 pH + E
0
Ag/AgCl

In the above-mentioned notation, E
RHE represents the potential vs RHE, E
Ag/AgCl is the potential measured when the reference electrode was Ag/AgCl, 0.059 is the correction factor, pH here, represents the pH of the electrolyte, E
0
Ag/AgCl is the standard thermodynamic potential of the Ag/AgCl i.e. 0.197.

The kinetics of the electrocatalyst depends solely on its ability to catalyse the chemical reaction. Good catalysts show excellent efficiency and their kinetics are a simple description of any of established mechanisms. So, catalytic behaviour has a great impact on the kinetics or vice versa. In current case, efficiency and kinetics of the prepared materials was evaluated by plotting a graph between overpotential (η) vs logj and considering the linear region of the steady state polarization curve to take Tafel slope. The equation for this purpose is as under:
(2)
η =
ɑ
+ (2·303RT/
αnF
) × log
j

In this equation, η represents the overpotential, α is the charge transfer coefficient, n is the number of electrons playing role in the reaction mechanism, F is the Faraday constant and j is the current density.

Turnover Frequency (TOF) is another parameter to evaluate the catalytic efficiency of a material for OER. It demonstrates the ability of a catalyst to convert the substrate under given conditions to product in unit time. Following equation is used to find out TOF of the synthesized material.
(3)
TOF = I/4 × F × m

In the above demonstration, I represents the current in amperes (A) at a specific overpotential (in current case η = 320 mV), F is the Faraday constant and m is the number of moles of active catalyst.

It is also an important parameter to assess the catalytic potential of the material under observation. The narrow potential window which was from 0 to 0.1 from the non-faradic region was selected from the cyclic voltammogram. CV data for that selected region was recorded at various scan rates i.e. 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30 and 35 mV s−1. From those plots, we calculated the difference between the anodic and cathodic current values. Those values were drawn against the already applied scan rates; consequently, we got a straight line. When slope of the line was divided by 2, double layer capacitance (Cdl) was achieved. When Cdl was divided by the specific capacitance (from literature review, the value of specific capacitance ~0.040 mF cm−2 was considered for flat electrode [61]), value of ECSA was obtained.

Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) is an important parameter to find the resistivity or the conductivity of the electrocatalyst. It is also essential to evaluate the solution resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the electrocatalyst-electrolyte interface. The EIS studies were carried out using Metrohm Autolab electrochemical workstation at an applied potential of 0.5 V. The frequency range was 1 × 105 − 0.1 Hz while the amplitude was 0.01 VRMS. Randles circuit was manipulated to acquire the values of Rs and Rct. Low values of Rct supported the high conductivity of the material and faster transfer of the electron.

A good catalyst should not only have greater activity but it should also maintain it for a longer period of time. Its high stability makes it appropriate for replacing the costly catalysts thus paving the way for its commercialization. Through chronoamperometry, the stability of materials can be verified. The chronoamperometry of the sample was performed at a potential of 0.8 V for about 40 hrs.

The phase purity and crystallinity of the synthesized samples were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. The powder XRD patterns of CDs, MOF and CDs@MOF were shown in Fig. 1(a–c), respectively. XRD pattern of CDs showed amorphous form while MOF and CDs@MOF were in crystalline form. CDs revealed a broad peak at 2θ = 20°, that was endorsed due to turbostratic carbon phase as reported previously [62]. It was further observed that the broad peak of CDs was converted into sharp peak owing to spectacular dispersion of CDs in DMF during composite synthesis and finally constituting the crystalline composite. The pre-dominant structure of composite contained the prominent peaks of CDs as well as sharp peaks of the MOF (# and * represent CDs and MOF, respectively) indicating successful formation of CDs@MOF as shown in Fig. 1c. as well as in inset. The average crystallite size of CDs, MOF and CDs@MOF were estimated to be 16 nm, 18 nm and 12 nm, respectively by using Scherer’s formula.

The FT-IR spectra of BTC, MOF, CDs and CDs@MOF were analysed and shown in Fig. 2a–d, respectively. The BTC FTIR spectrum showed a sharp peak at 1,746 cm−1 which correspond to the carbonyl group as shown in Fig. 2a. The disappearance of carbonyl group sharp peak of the linker BTC at 1,746 cm−1 (Fig. 2b) explained the de-protonation of –COOH group in MOF. That statement was further supported with the appearance of anti-symmetric stretching peak at 1,637 cm−1 and symmetric stretching peak at 1370 cm−1 of –COO− ion in MOF, respectively. The appearance of these two peaks and disappearance of strong absorption peak at 1,746 cm−1 endorsed the coordination between metal and ligand upon reaction with metal. A strong absorption peak at 487 cm−1 corresponds to bending mode of vibrations Ni–O bonds in MOF as shown in Fig. 2b. FT-IR spectra showed that the hydroxyl group of CDs has a broad absorption peak at approximately 3,460 cm−1 (Fig. 2c), while the hydroxyl group in CDs@MOF has a broad absorption peak at approximately 3,319 cm−1 (Fig. 2d) shifted downward, which indicates that there are intermolecular hydrogen bonds between the CDs and MOF.

The general morphologies for the CDs, MOF and CDs@MOF composite were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), depicted in Fig. 3(a–c), respectively. All the synthesized samples showed uniform and smooth surfaces. As clear from Fig. 3a–b, CDs had globular shape and MOF had rod shaped morphology. While in CDs@MOF nano-composite, CDs were decorated on MOF rods which can be clearly seen in inset of Fig. 3c. The particle diameters of CDs, MOF and CDs@MOF were calculated from SEM micrographs which were 5 nm, 4 nm and 3 nm, respectively which is in good agreement with that calculated from XRD data. Some of the particles were agglomerated into bigger particles as well.

Fig. S1a–c showed the EDX spectra of all the synthesized materials. The EDX confirmed the presence of respective elements. The percentage obtained from EDX analysis of all the samples were tabulated in Table S1. Thus, the successful assembly of CDs on the surface of MOF confirmed from SEM images and EDX spectra of CDs@MOF nano-composite.

Fig. S2(a–c) depicts the particle size analysis extracted from SEM images. The particles with size distribution existed in the range of 3–9 nm, 2–10 nm and 1–8 nm for CDs, MOF and CDs@MOF, respectively as evidenced from these results, most particles existed in the range of 3–5 nm.

Porous texture (specific surface area and pore size distribution) of MOF and CDs@MOF nano-composite were studied using nitrogen adsorption analysis. The BET surface area, pore size and pore volume of MOF and CDs@MOF were tabulated in Table 1. MOF exhibited smaller surface area than the CDs@MOF due to large crystallites size. The large pore volume and greater BET surface area of CDs@MOF nano-composite make it an excellent candidate for catalytic applications.

Electrocatalytic performances of the bare nickel foam (NF without catalyst loading), CDs, MOF and CDs@MOF loaded NFs were evaluated for OER in a typical three electrodes system in 1 M KOH at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. A current density of 10 mV cm−2, mandatory for the 10% efficiency of the solar to fuel conversion systems, was taken as one of the benchmarks for evaluating the electrocatalytic efficiency of the as-synthesized materials [63]. During electrochemical studies, all the working electrodes deposited with CDs, MOF, CDs@MOF and bare NF, displayed a pre-oxidation peak at about 1.37 V vs. RHE in the cyclic voltammogram as shown in Fig. 4a. As all the Ni-based materials here displayed prominent peaks before initiating the oxidation of water at around potential of 1.37 V vs. RHE during their oxidative scans, that could be because of the redox reaction of Ni2+ to Ni3+ species. This conversion of Ni2+ to Ni3+ species is initiation step of the sluggish OER which is the rate determining step of the water splitting. The presence and the high intensity of the oxidation peak further confirmed the abundance of highly active nickel species which expedited the lethargic oxidation of water in a refined way. It was further supported by the huge number of active sites and enormous electrochemical active surface area [61,63–65]. After this peak, an almost vertical peak was observed. This peak started to appear at a potential of 1.50 V vs. RHE representing the onset potential of the electrocatalyst. Onset potential is the potential where OER began and it was accompanied by the evolution of tiny O2 bubbles coming out of the surface of composite coated NF electrode during the oxidative scan. Forward oxidative scan having the overlapping values for the redox reaction of Ni2+ to Ni3+ species and onset potential could create misunderstanding. To avoid this uncertainty, reverse scan was used to calculate the values of onset and over potentials. The composite took an overpotential of 1.55 V (320 mV) vs. RHE to attain the benchmark current density of 10 mV cm−2. The peaks related to OER were also observed in case of bare NF, CDs and MOF loaded NF but their overpotential values were very high. CDs and MOF achieved the current density of 10 mV cm−2 at overpotential values of 1.76 V (530 mV) vs. RHE and 1.70 V (470 mV) vs. RHE, respectively. The values of onset and over potential of the as-synthesized composite were superior to many of the already reported MOF based electrocatalysts ( Table 2) [4,5,8,11,66–69]. As the value of potential elevated, the catalyst attained a current density of 50 mV cm−2 at overpotential of 1.65 V vs. RHE. It also managed to acquire the current density as high as 312 mV cm−2 at overpotential of 2.04 V vs. RHE.

Moreover, to deeply understand the electrochemical kinetics of the sluggish OER and to analyze the inherent electrocatalytic activity of the material, Tafel slope studies were carried out considering the linear region of the steady state polarization curve. The Tafel slope values for bare NF, CDs, MOF and CDs@MOF were 185 mV dec−1, 95 mV dec−1, 118 mV dec−1 and 62 mV dec−1 respectively (Fig. 4b). The lower value of Tafel slope of composite was much better than many already reported MOF based electrocatalysts (Table 2) and was comparable to the state of the art materials for OER i.e. 80 mV dec−1, 92.7 mV dec−1 for IrO2 and RuO2 @NF respectively [65]. This lower value further confirmed the easy and quick electron-proton transfer at the electrode-electrocatalyst interfaces. From this value of Tafel slope i.e. 62 mV dec−1, it became obvious that the mechanism for OER was proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET). During this mechanism, oxidation of one electron is accompanied by the transfer of one proton simultaneously. During electrocatalysis, an early and quick shift of electron would lead to the greater efficiency of the catalysts and lower would be the value of Tafel slope. Generally, OER goes through the formation of OH- intermediates and involves the 4 proton-electron transfer mechanism steps which are shown below (Eqs. (4)–(7)):
(4)
M + OH
-
↔
M - OH
-
+ e
-
(5)
M -
OH
-
+
OH
-
↔ M - O +
H
+
+
e
-
(6)
M − O +
OH
−
( l )
↔ M − OOH +
e
−
(7)
M − OOH +
OH
−
↔ M +
O
2
( g )
+
H
+
+
e
−
All the above information supported the excellent interaction of the electrocatalyst-electrolyte interfaces during OER.

In addition to the Tafel slope, low value of charge-transfer resistance (Rct) obtained from semicircle in low frequency zone via electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was also associated with the reaction kinetics that happened at the interface of the electrocatalyst and electrolyte. Rct values for CDs, MOF and CDs@MOF composite were 19.696 Ω, 24.43 Ω and 4.335 Ω, respectively ( Fig. 5). The values of solution resistance (Rs) are 2.33 Ω, 2.6 Ω and 2.1 Ω for the CDs@MOF composite, MOF and CDs respectively. The lower value of Rct and lesser diameter of the semicircle of the composite as compared to CDs and MOF were in agreement with the faster transfer of electron [70]. These values simply demonstrated that incorporation of CDs into MOF greatly decreased the resistance and resultantly enhanced the conductivity of the composite material which helped in easy transfer of electrons.

The combination of the CDs into MOF not only reduced the resistance and improved the conductance of the composite greatly but also heightened its electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) spectacularly. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) of the material has been measured at different scan rates ( Fig. 6), calculated the electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) and electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) was determined. ECSA values for CDs, MOF and CDs@MOF composite were 33.33 cm2, 66.6 cm2 and 216.6 cm2, respectively. Indeed, larger electrochemical active surface area as well as the BET surface area kept enormous electrochemical active sites which ultimately resulted superior activity towards OER.

Efficiency of the electrocatalyst was also demonstrated by the calculation of the Turnover frequency (TOF) which was another crucial parameter to evaluate the competence of electrocatalyst. TOF refers to capability of a catalyst to convert given number of moles of substrate under given condition in a unit time. Higher the value of TOF greater will be the efficiency of the catalyst. The composite under study displayed a TOF of 1.8 s−1 which was remarkable and much higher than that of the constituents i.e. MOF (0.007 s−1) and CDs (0.000049 s−1).

Despite superior activity, stability is another vital parameter that an electrocatalyst must fulfil to be used on commercial scale. The as-synthesized composite not only showed greater catalytic activity but also remained stable when tested during CV for up to 1,000 cycles at 50 mV s−1 in 1 M KOH solution. There was a minute change between the cyclic voltammograms of the 1st one, 500th and the 1,000th cycles as showed in Fig. 7a.

Stability and durability were further confirmed with the help of chronoamperometery technique. Continuous electrolysis for 40 hrs in 1 M KOH solution was carried out at 1.6 V vs. RHE. Time vs. current density curve obtained during the electrolysis showed that activity remained almost unchanged with a little decay over the course of time. Higher and durable catalytic performance of the as-synthesized material is attributed to the synergistic effect between CDs and MOF, shown in Fig. 7b.

A composite of CDs with Ni-based mixed linkers MOF containing BTC and bpy was successfully synthesized through one of the finest and easiest solvothermal reaction method. The distinctive synthetic route used in this work features low cost raw materials and simple methodology. The composite not only illustrated comparable onset i.e. 1.50 V vs. RHE and overpotential i.e. 1.55 V (320 mV) vs. RHE with other state of the art MOF based electrocatalysts but also demonstrated excellent stability. It remained stable up to 1,000 cycles during cyclic voltammetric scans and showed only a little decay when run for over a long period of time i.e. for 40 h. It also offered very little Tafel slope of 62 mV dec−1, small TOF of 1.8 s−1, low resistance of 4.335 Ω and greater ECSA of 216.6 cm−2. All these parameters made our composite a potential candidate for harnessing and exploiting the largest source i.e. water, on earth.

Muhammad Yousaf ur Rehman: Visualization, he worked in the laboratory i.e. experimental work done by him and also wrote the manuscript. Sumaira Manzoor, Nosheen Nazar and Abdul Ghafoor Abid: Development or design of methodology; creation of models. Ashfaq Mahmood Qureshi: Supervision. Adeel Hussain Chughtai: Synthesis of MOF and Composites, Writing - review & editing. Afzal Shah: Analysis of the materials i.e. XRD, SEM and EDX. Khurram Saleem Joya: Writing - review & editing. Muhammad Naeem Ashiq: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors (M. Y. Rehman and M. N. Ashiq) acknowledge Bahauddin Zakariya University, Multan Pakistan for financial support via grant no. DR&EL/D-545.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2020.158038.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface-assembled non-noble metal nanoscale Ni-colloidal thin-films as efficient electrocatalysts for water oxidation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Ul Ain Babar, A. Saddiqa, L. Nisar, S.R. Gilani, K.S. Joya, Surface-assembled non-noble metal nanoscale Ni-colloidal thin-films as efficient electrocatalysts for water oxidation, RSC Advances, 9 (2019) 37274‐37286.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hollow bimetallic cobalt-based selenide polyhedrons derived from metal–organic framework: an efficient bifunctional electrocatalyst for overall water splitting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, F. Li, W. Li, W. Gao, Y. Tang, R. Li, Hollow bimetallic cobalt-based selenide polyhedrons derived from metal–organic framework: an efficient bifunctional electrocatalyst for overall water splitting, Journal of Materials Chemistry A, 5 (2017) 17982‐17989.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step MOF-derived Co/Co9S8 nanoparticles embedded in nitrogen, sulfur and oxygen ternary-doped porous carbon: an efficient electrocatalyst for overall water splitting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Du, R. Wang, Y.-R. Lv, Y.-L. Wei, S.-Q. Zang, One-step MOF-derived Co/Co 9 S 8 nanoparticles embedded in nitrogen, sulfur and oxygen ternary-doped porous carbon: an efficient electrocatalyst for overall water splitting, Chemical Communications, 55 (2019) 3203‐3206.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal–organic framework-based CoP/reduced graphene oxide: high-performance bifunctional electrocatalyst for overall water splitting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Jiao, Y.-X. Zhou, H.-L. Jiang, Metal–organic framework-based CoP/reduced graphene oxide: high-performance bifunctional electrocatalyst for overall water splitting, Chemical science, 7 (2016) 1690‐1695.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mn and S dual-doping of MOF-derived Co3O4 electrode array increases the efficiency of electrocatalytic generation of oxygen

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Qi, H. Wang, J. Lin, C. Li, X. Si, J. Cao, Z. Zhong, J. Feng, Mn and S dual-doping of MOF-derived Co3O4 electrode array increases the efficiency of electrocatalytic generation of oxygen, Journal of colloid and interface science, 557 (2019) 28‐33.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Co-doping strategy for developing perovskite oxides as highly efficient electrocatalysts for oxygen evolution reaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Xu, C. Su, W. Zhou, Y. Zhu, Y. Chen, Z. Shao, Co-doping Strategy for Developing Perovskite Oxides as Highly Efficient Electrocatalysts for Oxygen Evolution Reaction, Advanced Science, 3 (2016) 1500187.
